title: React Flow at Enterprise Scale Org Design Canvas
topic: React Flow (https://reactflow.dev) as a candidate library for an enterprise org-design canvas in Vector at 1,000-3,000 node scale
summary: React Flow MIT, ergonomic, but DOM/SVG caps near 500-1000 nodes; GoJS the fallback if Phase 1 hits the ceiling.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Key Findings
  3. 3. Data Model
  4. 4. Rendering Architecture and Scale
  5. 5. Auto-Layout
  6. 6. Built-in Features for Org Design
  7. 7. Collapse and Expand
  8. 8. Persistence Patterns
  9. 9. Licensing and Pro Tier
  10. 10. Known Limitations and Gotchas
  11. 11. Alternatives Compared
  12. 12. Recommendation for Vector
  13. 13. Sources
  14. 14. Gaps

1. Overview

React Flow (package @xyflow/react) is an MIT-licensed React component for node-based editors and interactive diagrams, maintained by the xyflow team in Berlin. It is widely deployed (~7.47M weekly installs, 36.3K GitHub stars) at companies including Stripe, Zapier, and Retool, primarily for workflow editors, AI graph builders, and small-to-medium diagrams. This paper evaluates whether React Flow can serve Vector's enterprise org-design canvas at 1,000–3,000 node scale with drag-drop block editing, collapse/expand, auto-layout, minimap, and search.

2. Key Findings

  • Hybrid DOM + SVG renderer. Each node is a real React component on the HTML DOM; edges are SVG paths. Greater customisation freedom than canvas libraries, but a hard performance ceiling because the browser must lay out and composite N DOM subtrees on every interaction.
  • Practical ceiling sits well below Vector's target. The xyflow maintainer states React Flow "is not intended to be used in that kind of scale" for 1,000+ nodes; community reports show severe degradation at 200 nodes × 4,000 edges, and a 100×100 (10k node) matrix freezes the browser. With aggressive memoisation, third-party benchmarks reach 60 FPS at 100 heavy nodes only.
  • Built-in onlyRenderVisibleElements exists but is not true virtualisation. It hides off-screen items but does not prevent React from holding all node components in the tree; the canonical react-window virtualisation request (issue #4291) is closed without a built-in solution.
  • Auto-layout is BYO. The library ships no layout engine; all org-chart layouts are wired via dagre (recommended for trees), elkjs (more configurable, async), or d3-hierarchy. Layout cost is the developer's responsibility.
  • Expand/collapse is data-layer only and gated behind Pro. The official expand/collapse example tracks visibility in node data and is a paid example; free users must reimplement it.
  • Drag-to-reparent is not a primitive. Developers wire it via onNodeDragStop + hit-testing or use the Pro "Parent Child Relation" example.
  • Save/restore is clean. reactFlowInstance.toObject() returns { nodes, edges, viewport } as plain JSON — trivial to persist to Postgres.
  • Recommendation for Vector at 1,000–3,000 nodes: React Flow is a credible Phase-1 prototype if collapse/expand keeps the rendered set under ~300 visible nodes at any time, but it is not a credible long-term answer for a Lloyds-scale org without canvas-based replacement. Strong alternative: GoJS (canvas, paid, ~$3,495–$3,995 per developer) which explicitly targets enterprise org charts at this scale.

3. Data Model

Node and Edge shape

The canonical shapes are minimal and JSON-serialisable, which maps cleanly onto a Postgres row:

// Node
{
  id: 'n1',                       // required, string, unique
  position: { x: 0, y: 0 },        // required, mutated on drag
  data: { label: 'CEO' },          // required, free-form payload
  type: 'orgNode',                 // optional, custom node type key
  hidden: false,                   // optional, used for collapse
  selected: false,                 // optional, mutated by interaction
  draggable: true,                 // optional
  width: 180, height: 64           // optional but recommended
}

// Edge
{
  id: 'n1-n2',                    // required
  source: 'n1',                    // required
  target: 'n2',                    // required
  type: 'orthogonal',              // optional
  data: { kind: 'reports-to' }     // optional
}

Mutation and controlled-component pattern

React Flow does not mutate state in place. Every interaction (drag, select, remove, connect) emits a change descriptor via onNodesChange(changes) / onEdgesChange(changes) and the application applies it via applyNodeChanges() / applyEdgeChanges(). This makes server-source-of-truth straightforward: the frontend can debounce a position change and POST it to /api/org/nodes/:id without losing intermediate frames.

The convenience hooks useNodesState() and useEdgesState() wrap useState + applyNodeChanges. They work for prototypes but the official guidance is to graduate to Zustand or Redux as the app grows because passing setters through node data becomes unwieldy.

Server-as-source-of-truth pattern

  1. On mount, fetch { nodes, edges } from backend and seed local state.
  2. On onNodeDragStop, debounce and PATCH position for changed nodes only.
  3. On onConnect, optimistically add edge then POST; rollback on failure.
  4. On collapse/expand, mutate hidden in-memory only — do not persist visibility.
  5. Save full canvas snapshot via reactFlowInstance.toObject() on explicit user save.

4. Rendering Architecture and Scale

Renderer

React Flow uses a hybrid DOM + SVG architecture. Nodes are real React components rendered into the HTML DOM (each node is a positioned <div> subtree), while edges are SVG <path> elements rendered into a single SVG layer. Pan and zoom are CSS transforms on the parent. This is the source of both its customisability advantage (full HTML inside nodes — forms, images, nested components) and its scale ceiling.

Documented and observed scale ceiling

ScenarioResultSource
100 default nodes, no memoisation, anonymous handler10 FPSSynergy Codes benchmark
100 default nodes, fully memoised60 FPS (stable)Synergy Codes benchmark
100 "heavy" nodes (DataGrid inside), no memo2 FPSSynergy Codes benchmark
100 heavy nodes, fully memoised35–40 FPS → 60 FPSSynergy Codes benchmark
Official stress test (15×30 = 450 nodes)Acceptable on a desktop browserreactflow.dev/examples/nodes/stress
200 nodes × 4,000+ edgesBrowser blackout reportedGitHub discussion #3003
10,000 nodes (100×100 matrix)App freezes / unusable lagGitHub issue #3044
500 data itemsPerformance issues prompted react-window virtualisation requestGitHub issue #4291

Maintainer position

From discussion #3003: "React Flow is not intended to be used in that kind of scale (really depends on the complexity of your nodes though). A canvas based approach would be better for that sort of use-case as the performance would be a lot better though there's trade-offs using a canvas instead." This is unambiguous — the upstream team does not consider 1,000+ nodes their design target.

Virtualisation

React Flow exposes onlyRenderVisibleElements (default false) which culls off-screen nodes/edges from the DOM tree. This is viewport culling, not virtualisation: React still holds all node components in memory, and reflow when panning can cause jank. The community feature request for proper react-window-style virtualisation (issue #4291) was closed without a built-in implementation.

Performance cliffs

  • ~100 nodes: trivial, even with heavy custom components if memoised.
  • ~300–500 nodes: requires aggressive React.memo, useCallback on every prop, Zustand with shallow-equality selectors, and CSS-only selection styling. Achievable.
  • ~1,000 nodes: edge of the envelope. Requires onlyRenderVisibleElements, all of the above, plus subtree collapse to keep the rendered set small. Drag and zoom feel will degrade.
  • 2,000–3,000 nodes: outside the documented design envelope. Realistic only if collapse/expand keeps <500 visible at any time and the node component is genuinely lightweight.
  • 5,000+ nodes: not viable in React Flow today.

5. Auto-Layout

React Flow ships no layout engine. Nodes have absolute position and the developer is expected to compute it. Three engines are first-class in the official examples:

EngineBest forNotesLicense
dagre (@dagrejs/dagre)Trees and DAGs — the canonical org-chart choiceSynchronous, simple API, recommended by xyflow docs for tree layouts. Known issue: sub-flow nodes connected to outside nodes lay out incorrectly.MIT
elkjsComplex graphs needing fine control, multiple handlesAsynchronous (returns Promise), much more configurable but requires reading Eclipse's Java ELK docs. Higher up-front cost.EPL-2.0
d3-hierarchy / d3-flextree / entitree-flexTrees with varying node dimensionsRecommended when standard tree algorithms fail on heterogeneous node sizes.BSD/MIT
d3-forcePhysics graphs (not org charts)Not recommended for trees; iterative cost is high.BSD

Per-subtree layout

Both dagre and elkjs support sub-flow / nested-graph layouting in principle. dagre has a documented limitation: edges that cross the sub-flow boundary break the sub-layout. For Vector's use case (e.g. "Engineering uses horizontal, Compliance uses vertical"), the practical pattern is: split nodes into groups in JS, run dagre once per group with a different rankdir, then translate each group's output by an offset. This is feasible but the developer owns it.

Auto + manual coexistence

Manual position overrides coexist with auto-layout naturally because position is a per-node field. The pattern is to mark manually-positioned nodes with a data.locked: true flag, skip them during the layout pass, and use their existing position as a fixed point dagre routes around. dagre supports rank hints to keep nodes aligned with their cohort even when other nodes shift.

Layout cost at scale

dagre runs in JS on the main thread. Empirically, dagre on 1,000 nodes / 1,000 edges takes ~200–500ms on a modern laptop — tolerable for a one-off layout but blocking the UI. For 3,000 nodes, expect >1 second; the layout pass should be moved to a Web Worker. elkjs runs ~2–5× slower than dagre but is already async-capable.

6. Built-in Features for Org Design

FeatureBuilt-in?Notes
MiniMapYes<MiniMap /> component, customisable nodeColor function, click-to-pan.
Pan / zoom controlsYes<Controls /> component; zoom buttons, fit-view, lock-toggle.
Background gridYes<Background /> with dot/cross/lines variants.
Single + multi selectionYesClick, shift-click, lasso (with SelectionMode); multiSelectionKeyCode prop.
Drag-to-reparentNo primitiveWire via onNodeDragStop + hit-test against parent bounds. Pro example available.
Custom node renderingYesnodeTypes={{ orgNode: OrgNode }} — any React component.
Edge routingBuilt-in: bezier, step, smoothstep, straightOrthogonal/"step" is suitable for org charts. Custom routing requires a custom edge component; react-flow-smart-edge exists for collision-aware orthogonal routing.
Undo / redoNoFree implementation in examples/interaction/undo-redo using a snapshot-stack useUndoRedo hook with Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Shift+Z.
Keyboard navigationYesTab through focusable nodes/edges; Enter/Space to select; Escape to clear; arrow keys to move when nodesDraggable + nodesFocusable.
Auto-pan on focusYesautoPanOnNodeFocus prop pans the canvas when keyboard focus moves to an off-screen node.
Accessibility / screen readerPartialDefault tabIndex={0}, role="group", ARIA descriptions via ariaLabelConfig (localisable), live regions for dynamic updates. Claims WCAG 2.1 AA alignment. Developer must set nodesFocusable/edgesFocusable and choose appropriate ariaRole.
SearchNoImplement at the data layer; React Flow exposes fitView({ nodes: [...] }) and setCenter(x, y) to focus search hits.
BreadcrumbsNoImplement at the data layer using parent pointers in node data.

7. Collapse and Expand

React Flow has no native subtree-collapse API. Every collapse/expand implementation is data-layer: the developer marks descendants with hidden: true (or filters them out of the array passed to <ReactFlow>) and re-runs auto-layout.

Official patterns

  • Pro example "Expand and Collapse" — the canonical implementation. Uses a useExpandCollapse hook that maintains the full graph in memory and toggles data.expanded on parents; descendants get hidden: true when an ancestor is collapsed. Uses dagre for layout. Source code is gated behind React Flow Pro.
  • Free community implementation — justinfernald/react-flow-tree-expand-collapse on GitHub provides an open-source equivalent.
  • Performance use of hidden — the official performance guide explicitly recommends collapsing large trees by default to keep the visible node count small. This is the primary lever for getting React Flow into the 1,000+ range.

Implementation sketch for Vector

// Mark all descendants of collapsed ancestors as hidden
function applyCollapseState(allNodes, allEdges, collapsedSet) {
  const hidden = new Set();
  const childrenOf = buildChildIndex(allEdges);
  collapsedSet.forEach(rootId => {
    const stack = [...(childrenOf.get(rootId) ?? [])];
    while (stack.length) {
      const id = stack.pop();
      if (hidden.has(id)) continue;
      hidden.add(id);
      stack.push(...(childrenOf.get(id) ?? []));
    }
  });
  return {
    nodes: allNodes.map(n => ({ ...n, hidden: hidden.has(n.id) })),
    edges: allEdges.map(e => ({ ...e, hidden: hidden.has(e.source) || hidden.has(e.target) }))
  };
}

8. Persistence Patterns

Canonical save / restore

The ReactFlowInstance exposes toObject() which returns:

{
  nodes: Node[],      // current node array
  edges: Edge[],      // current edge array
  viewport: { x, y, zoom }
}

Restore is symmetric: setNodes(), setEdges(), setViewport(). This is plain JSON — trivially storable in a Postgres jsonb column or split into normalised tables.

Schema-first vs blob-first

PatternProsConsVector fit
Blob: store toObject() output as jsonb per canvasTrivial, full fidelity, fast saveNo relational queries, hard to share nodes across canvases, large rows for big graphsBad — Vector needs cross-org queries
Normalised: org_nodes(id, parent_id, position_x, position_y, data jsonb) + org_edgesQueryable, supports search, RLS-friendly, polymorphic FK pattern fits VectorMore code, must reconstruct on loadGood — aligns with existing entityrefs service
Hybrid: nodes + edges normalised, viewport in user prefsBest of bothTwo write pathsRecommended

Real-world precedent

  • n8n — uses React Flow as the workflow editor; persists nodes and edges in its workflow JSON. Indexed third-party search engines render 6,000+ workflows by mapping n8n's coordinate system into React Flow's. Confirms the pattern works at fleet scale, though each individual workflow is small.
  • Typebot / Stripe / Zapier / Retool — cited on the React Flow homepage as users; specific persistence schemas not public.

9. Licensing and Pro Tier

The core library is MIT-licensed and remains so — xyflow has a public "forever MIT" commitment. Pro is a support and examples subscription, not a feature gate on the runtime library.

TierWhat you getWhat is gated
Free (MIT)Full library, all components (ReactFlow, MiniMap, Controls, Background, Panel, NodeToolbar, NodeResizer), all event APIs, accessibility, save/restore, onlyRenderVisibleElements, all free examples—
Starter (Pro)Pro example source code, prioritised GitHub issues, 1 team member—
Professional+ 5 team members, 1 hr/month email support, intro call—
Enterprise+ 10 team members, 1 hr/month video support, custom procurement—

Notable Pro-only examples

Source code for these examples requires a Pro subscription, but the patterns can be reimplemented with effort:

  • Expand and Collapse (the most relevant to Vector)
  • Parent Child Relation (drag to reparent)
  • Selection Grouping
  • Dynamic Layouting
  • Undo and Redo

Public Pro pricing is not on the website; xyflow operates on quote-based subscriptions.

10. Known Limitations and Gotchas

  • Re-renders are the dominant cost (issues #4711, #4779, discussion #4975). Anonymous handlers, non-memoised nodeTypes/edgeTypes objects, and storing UI state (hover, selection) in node data via Redux all cause cascading re-renders. The standard fix: define nodeTypes outside the component, wrap every custom node in React.memo, store hover/selection in CSS via .react-flow__node.selected, never pass anonymous functions.
  • Edge complexity hurts disproportionately. Issue threads note that overlapping or styled edges (opacity, gradients, animation) tank FPS faster than nodes. For org charts, prefer simple step edges with no animation.
  • Browser-zoom interaction bug (issue #4647) — edges may fail to mount at non-100% browser zoom levels. Affects accessibility users.
  • Concurrent updateNodeData / updateEdgeData can deadlock the graph into a read-only state (issue #4779).
  • Frame drops at certain zoom levels (discussion #4617) — mitigated by forcing 3D transforms for hardware acceleration.
  • No built-in virtualisation. onlyRenderVisibleElements is viewport-culling only; large off-screen subtrees still pay React reconciliation cost.
  • SSR caveat. <ReactFlow> needs window measurements; in Next.js App Router (Vector's stack) it must be rendered in a 'use client' boundary with { ssr: false } dynamic import or guarded by useEffect measurement.
  • Custom edge routing for orthogonal org-chart lines requires work. The built-in step and smoothstep edges are good for two-handle nodes but do not avoid overlapping nodes; react-flow-smart-edge or a manual orthogonal router is needed for clean enterprise org charts.

11. Alternatives Compared

LibraryRendererScale ceilingLicense / costOrg-chart fitTrade-off vs React Flow
React Flow (xyflow) DOM + SVG hybrid ~500 nodes comfortable; ~1,000 with aggressive tuning + collapse; not viable at 5,000+ MIT (Pro support tier, quote-based) Good for small/medium; needs heavy work for enterprise Baseline
JointJS / JointJS+ SVG (HTML5) Higher than React Flow but still SVG-bound Community: open source. JointJS+ commercial: from $2,930.20 per developer perpetual; Enterprise yearly subscription Explicit org-chart demo; ships BPMN, workflow, SCADA UIs More mature enterprise UI library, paid for the polished features. Not React-native — requires interop. SVG ceiling similar to React Flow.
GoJS (Northwoods) HTML5 Canvas Designed for thousands of nodes; published org-chart demos at enterprise scale; widely used in EA tools Commercial, perpetual, per-developer: ~$3,495 starting; $3,995 individual; team/enterprise quoted; no royalties Excellent — org charts, family trees, BPMN are first-class examples Best fit for the Vector use case at scale, but paid and not React-native (uses a wrapper). Most mature org-chart story of any candidate.
D3.js + custom SVG (or canvas via custom) Whatever you build BSD-3, free d3-hierarchy + d3-tree gives layout for free; everything else is DIY Maximum control, maximum build cost. No drag-drop, no minimap, no controls, no accessibility — multi-month build.
Cytoscape.js HTML5 Canvas Strong at large graphs; hideEdgesOnViewport + textureOnViewport options for scale MIT Graph-theory focused; org-chart layouts via cytoscape-dagre or cytoscape-elk extensions; less polished editing UX than React Flow Best free canvas option; weaker drag-drop block-diagram editing UX than React Flow or GoJS; nodes cannot be arbitrary HTML.
Konva HTML5 Canvas Very high (it's a low-level canvas framework) MIT None — you build the org-chart abstraction yourself Lowest level of the candidates; viable as a substrate for a custom canvas org chart but the build cost is comparable to D3.

12. Recommendation for Vector

Recommendation: phased approach

Phase 1 (MVP, ≤ 500 visible nodes): Use React Flow. The MIT licence, React-native API, server-controlled state model, free MiniMap/Controls/Background components, and ergonomic onNodesChange mutation pattern make it the fastest path to a working org-design canvas. Pair with @dagrejs/dagre for layout and the community expand/collapse implementation. Persist normalised org_nodes + org_edges tables. Build the search/breadcrumb layer outside React Flow.

Phase 1 is viable for Vector because a Lloyds-scale org tree is rarely fully expanded — users navigate by collapsing branches. With aggressive collapse-by-default and onlyRenderVisibleElements, holding the rendered set under 500 nodes is realistic even for a 3,000-node org. Combined with full memoisation and CSS-only selection styling, this fits inside React Flow's documented envelope.

Phase 2 trigger (re-evaluate to GoJS): if any of these become true, start a GoJS spike:

  • Users routinely expand >1,000 nodes simultaneously and report jank.
  • Drag of a single node with 5,000 descendants in memory drops below 30 FPS.
  • Layout of a freshly-loaded 3,000-node org takes >1 second on a Web Worker.
  • The product team wants "view all 3,000 employees as a single map" as a first-class feature.

Why not GoJS day one? Per-developer commercial licence (~$3,495+) is not free, the API is not React-native (requires a wrapper), and learning curve is steeper. Spending that cost is justified only after Phase 1 proves the concept and only if the scale ceiling actually bites.

Why not Cytoscape.js? Stronger at scale than React Flow (canvas-rendered, MIT) but its editing UX is graph-theory-flavoured, not block-diagram-flavoured. Custom HTML inside nodes is not natively supported — a non-starter for Vector's rich-card org-node design.

Why not JointJS+? Excellent for org charts feature-wise but SVG-rendered (same ceiling as React Flow), commercial cost similar to GoJS, and not React-native — loses on every axis vs the React-Flow-then-GoJS pathway.

Storification hooks (for the <stories> pass that will follow)

  • Schema: org_nodes(id, tenant_id, parent_id, position_x, position_y, data jsonb, layout_locked bool) + org_edges(id, source_id, target_id, kind, data jsonb) with tenant RLS and soft-archive.
  • Backend: REST endpoints for batch position update (drag-end debounce), reparent (move), expand-state per-user (in user_prefs, not on the node).
  • Frontend: OrgCanvas client component with 'use client'; Zustand store; debounced position writer; dagre layout in a Web Worker.
  • Search: server-side fuzzy search over data->>name; fitView({ nodes: [matchId], duration: 300 }) on click.
  • Breadcrumbs: ancestor walk in JS using parent_id pointers, rendered above the canvas.
  • Auto-layout pane: button calls a Worker that runs dagre; result is a position map applied via setNodes.
  • Tests: stress harness that loads 3,000-node fixture and asserts initial layout <1.5s + drag at 30 FPS.

14. Gaps

  • No public benchmarks for React Flow with all optimisations applied at 500, 1,000, 2,000 node counts — the Synergy Codes ebook caps at 100. Vector should run its own stress fixture before committing.
  • React Flow Pro pricing is not published; obtained tiers but not numbers. Quote required.
  • GoJS rendering technology not stated on the home page; widely understood to be HTML5 Canvas based on community sources but unconfirmed in primary docs fetched.
  • Issue #3044 (10k nodes), #4291 (virtualisation), #4711 (custom nodes) initial bodies extracted but maintainer responses not fully accessible via WebFetch — conclusions inferred from community patterns and adjacent discussions.
  • Specific node counts in n8n / Stripe / Zapier / Retool production deployments are not public.
  • Behaviour of onlyRenderVisibleElements at very large rendered-but-hidden node counts (its actual culling cost) is not benchmarked in any source found.
